title: Assault on Doomrock: Ultimate Edition - a detailed, casual unboxing and comparison
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2WaFFWc3lno

Opening 2024, Daniel (@dungeondive) does a long, casual unboxing of Assault on Doomrock: Ultimate Edition — but first addresses a controversy head-on: one US-edition card used a meme (the 'kek' flag) the designer says he didn't know had been co-opted by the alt-right, apologised for, and had meant to cut. Daniel reads the apology, notes the designer's earlier ASL 'I love you' mix-up, and chooses to give him the benefit of the doubt. Then the cardboard: this boardless game runs on big tokens, terrain and a point-crawl adventure across settlement, wilderness, mountain and boss location cards, spending time to explore, shop and level before three escalating battles. Combat stays gridless and tactical with dice allocation, interactive terrain, and semi-autonomous 'goons' for solo play. He's giddy about the separately-sold roguelike expansion — challenges that let you permanently upgrade loot and abilities, a light rogue-legacy layer. Comparing to the original, he notes bigger location cards, AI decks consolidated onto the battle mats, more classes and settings, and updated art, while hoping above all they've trimmed the notoriously long runtime. A game he loves for its sheer character-building variety.

----

Daniel chose to give Doomrock's designer the benefit of the doubt over the pulled card. When a creator owns a mistake and apologises, does that settle it for you, or does it still colour the game on your table?
